description:
'''UK underground rap''' (also referred to as '''UK underground''', '''UK UG''', or '''Overground''') is a [[hip-hop]] [[music genre]], [[counterculture]] and scene of [[UK rap]] that emerged in [[United Kingdom|the United Kingdom]] during the early 2020s. It has been called "the UK's rap revolution".   

The scene differs from earlier waves of [[Underground hip-hop|underground]] [[UK rap|UK hip-hop]] through its strong influence from [[Music of the United States|American]] [[internet rap]] genres such as [[cloud rap]], [[Plugg music|plugg]], [[Rage music|rage]] and [[Jerk (music genre)|jerk]].    

The genre is typically characterised by heavily [[Distortion (music)|distorted]], [[Bass (sound)|bass-heavy]] production built around clipped, burst [[Roland TR-808|808s]] and [[Snare drum|snare]]-heavy drum patterns. Beats are usually 130 to 160 [[Beats per minute|BPM]] and frequently feature distorted [[Lo-fi music|lo-fi]] [[Texture (music)|textures]] and backward [[Loop (music)|loops]], alongside sparse [[Melody|melodic elements]] and glassy, [[rompler]]-style [[digital piano]] and skittering percussion. Vocal delivery tends toward flat, [[monotone]] rapping over abrasive instrumentals, though some artists employ melodic, [[AutoTune]]d approaches. Lyrics are often introspective and vulnerable, and frequently mention pride in being [[Youth in the United Kingdom|young]] and British despite the [[United Kingdom cost-of-living crisis|difficult socioeconomic circumstances]] faced by [[Generation Z]].    

Much like the [[Britpop]] and [[Punk subculture|punk]] movements, the UK underground places [[British national identity|Britishness]] at the forefront, with many artists reclaiming [[Union Jack|the Union Jack]] and [[Culture of the United Kingdom|British cultural imagery]] as an act of defiance against the [[Far-right politics in the United Kingdom|British far-right]]'s [[Operation Raise the Colours|use of the Union Jack]] and [[2024 United Kingdom riots|rising anti-immigration rhetoric]]. Speaking with ''[[The Guardian]]'', director Lauzza added that young people were "creating our own Britain and our own culture that we can be proud of".   

Notable artists associated with the scene include [[EsDeeKid]], [[Fakemink]], Rico Ace, [[Fimiguerrero]], [[Feng (rapper)|Feng]], [[Lancey Foux]], [[Jim Legxacy]], and [[YT (rapper)|YT]].  

The scene has achieved significant [[critic]]al and commercial success, and much like the name "[[Indie rock|indie]]" rock, the term "underground" is used to refer to the genre [[Musical style|stylistically]] rather than denoting popularity.
